### Setup and Run React Native Example

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/react/react-native/README.md

These commands install the Expo CLI globally, then install project dependencies, and finally start the React Native development server.

```shell
npm install --global expo-cli
```

```shell
pnpm install
```

```shell
pnpm start
```

--------------------------------

### Start Svelte Development Server

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/svelte/auto-refetching/README.md

Launch a local development server after installing project dependencies. The second command automatically opens the application in a new browser tab for immediate preview and testing.

```bash
npm run dev
```

```bash
npm run dev -- --open
```

--------------------------------

### Run a specific example in development mode

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md

This command executes the development script for a selected example within its directory using pnpm. It starts the example application, allowing developers to test and interact with it locally.

```bash
pnpm run dev
```

--------------------------------

### Use Vue Query with Composition API and script setup

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/vue/installation.md

Example of using useQuery composable with Vue 3's <script setup> syntax. Demonstrates destructuring query state properties including isPending, isFetching, isError, data, and error from the useQuery hook.

```vue
<script setup>
import { useQuery } from '@tanstack/vue-query'

const { isPending, isFetching, isError, data, error } = useQuery({
  queryKey: ['todos'],
  queryFn: getTodos,
})
</script>

<template>...</template>
```

--------------------------------

### React Query Setup with Queries, Mutations, and Invalidation

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/quick-start.md

Complete React Query implementation demonstrating client initialization, data fetching with useQuery, data mutation with useMutation, and cache invalidation. This example shows how to fetch todos, display them in a list, and add new todos with automatic cache refresh. Requires @tanstack/react-query package and API functions (getTodos, postTodo).

```tsx
import {
  useQuery,
  useMutation,
  useQueryClient,
  QueryClient,
  QueryClientProvider,
} from '@tanstack/react-query'
import { getTodos, postTodo } from '../my-api'

// Create a client
const queryClient = new QueryClient()

function App() {
  return (
    // Provide the client to your App
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
      <Todos />
    </QueryClientProvider>
  )
}

function Todos() {
  // Access the client
  const queryClient = useQueryClient()

  // Queries
  const query = useQuery({ queryKey: ['todos'], queryFn: getTodos })

  // Mutations
  const mutation = useMutation({
    mutationFn: postTodo,
    onSuccess: () => {
      // Invalidate and refetch
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['todos'] })
    },
  })

  return (
    <div>
      <ul>
        {query.data?.map((todo) => (
          <li key={todo.id}>{todo.title}</li>
        ))}
      </ul>

      <button
        onClick={() => {
          mutation.mutate({
            id: Date.now(),
            title: 'Do Laundry',
          })
        }}
      >
        Add Todo
      </button>
    </div>
  )
}

render(<App />, document.getElementById('root'))
```

--------------------------------

### Basic Lit-Query Usage with createQueryController

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/packages/lit-query/README.md

A quick start example demonstrating how to set up `QueryClientProvider` and use `createQueryController` within a LitElement to fetch and display data.

```ts
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import {
  QueryClient,
  QueryClientProvider,
  createQueryController,
} from '@tanstack/lit-query'

const client = new QueryClient({
  defaultOptions: { queries: { retry: false } },
})

class AppProvider extends QueryClientProvider {
  constructor() {
    super()
    this.client = client
  }
}
customElements.define('app-provider', AppProvider)

class UsersView extends LitElement {
  private readonly users = createQueryController(this, {
    queryKey: ['users'],
    queryFn: async () => {
      const response = await fetch('/api/users')
      return response.json() as Promise<Array<{ id: string; name: string }>>
    },
  })

  render() {
    const query = this.users()
    if (query.isPending) return html`Loading...`
    if (query.isError) return html`Error`
    return html`<ul>
      ${query.data?.map((u) => html`<li>${u.name}</li>`)}
    </ul>`
  }
}
customElements.define('users-view', UsersView)
```

--------------------------------

### Set up and run TanStack website for local docs preview

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md

These commands navigate into the `tanstack.com` directory, install its dependencies using pnpm, and then start the development server. This allows previewing local changes made to the `TanStack/query` documentation on `http://localhost:3000`.

```sh
cd tanstack.com
pnpm i
pnpm dev
```

--------------------------------

### Start SolidStart Development Server

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/solid/solid-start-streaming/README.md

After installing dependencies, use this command to start the development server for a SolidStart project. The `npm run dev` command initiates the server, and an optional `--open` flag can be used to automatically open the application in a new browser tab. This is crucial for local development and testing.

```bash
npm run dev

# or start the server and open the app in a new browser tab
npm run dev -- --open
```

--------------------------------

### Start development server

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/svelte/playground/README.md

Run this command to start the local development environment.

```bash
npm run dev
```

--------------------------------

### Basic TanStack Query Setup with useQuery Hook

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/overview.md

Demonstrates the fundamental setup of TanStack Query including QueryClient initialization, QueryClientProvider wrapper, and useQuery hook for fetching GitHub repository data. The example shows handling loading states, errors, and displaying fetched data with proper TypeScript support.

```typescript
import {
  QueryClient,
  QueryClientProvider,
  useQuery,
} from '@tanstack/react-query'

const queryClient = new QueryClient()

export default function App() {
  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
      <Example />
    </QueryClientProvider>
  )
}

function Example() {
  const { isPending, error, data } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ['repoData'],
    queryFn: () =>
      fetch('https://api.github.com/repos/TanStack/query').then((res) =>
        res.json(),
      ),
  })

  if (isPending) return 'Loading...'

  if (error) return 'An error has occurred: ' + error.message

  return (
    <div>
      <h1>{data.name}</h1>
      <p>{data.description}</p>
      <strong>👀 {data.subscribers_count}</strong>{' '}
      <strong>✨ {data.stargazers_count}</strong>{' '}
      <strong>🍴 {data.forks_count}</strong>
    </div>
  )
}
```

--------------------------------

### Install dependencies

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/svelte/playground/README.md

Run this command to install all project dependencies.

```bash
npm install
```

--------------------------------

### Implement basic data fetching with SolidJS and TanStack Query

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/solid/quick-start.md

This example demonstrates how to integrate TanStack Query with SolidJS to fetch and display a list of todos. It showcases the use of `QueryClientProvider` to set up the query client, and `useQuery` to manage the asynchronous data fetching state, including pending, error, and success states.

```tsx
import {
  QueryClient,
  QueryClientProvider,
  useQuery,
} from '@tanstack/solid-query'
import { Switch, Match, For } from 'solid-js'

const queryClient = new QueryClient()

function Example() {
  const query = useQuery(() => ({
    queryKey: ['todos'],
    queryFn: fetchTodos,
  }))

  return (
    <div>
      <Switch>
        <Match when={query.isPending}>
          <p>Loading...</p>
        </Match>
        <Match when={query.isError}>
          <p>Error: {query.error.message}</p>
        </Match>
        <Match when={query.isSuccess}>
          <For each={query.data}>{(todo) => <p>{todo.title}</p>}</For>
        </Match>
      </Switch>
    </div>
  )
}

function App() {
  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
      <Example />
    </QueryClientProvider>
  )
}
```

--------------------------------

### Basic Data Fetching and Mutation with TanStack Vue Query

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/vue/quick-start.md

This example demonstrates how to use useQuery for fetching data and useMutation for updating data in a Vue component. It includes error and loading state handling, as well as cache invalidation using useQueryClient after a successful mutation.

```vue
<script setup>
import { useQueryClient, useQuery, useMutation } from '@tanstack/vue-query'

// Access QueryClient instance
const queryClient = useQueryClient()

// Query
const { isPending, isError, data, error } = useQuery({
  queryKey: ['todos'],
  queryFn: getTodos,
})

// Mutation
const mutation = useMutation({
  mutationFn: postTodo,
  onSuccess: () => {
    // Invalidate and refetch
    queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['todos'] })
  },
})

function onButtonClick() {
  mutation.mutate({
    id: Date.now(),
    title: 'Do Laundry',
  })
}
</script>

<template>
  <span v-if="isPending">Loading...</span>
  <span v-else-if="isError">Error: {{ error.message }}</span>
  <!-- We can assume by this point that `isSuccess === true` -->
  <ul v-else>
    <li v-for="todo in data" :key="todo.id">{{ todo.title }}</li>
  </ul>
  <button @click="onButtonClick">Add Todo</button>
</template>
```

--------------------------------

### Install Vue Query via bun

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/vue/installation.md

Install @tanstack/vue-query package using bun package manager, a modern JavaScript runtime and package manager.

```bash
bun add @tanstack/vue-query
```

```bash
npm run dev

# or start the server and open the app in a new browser tab
npm run dev -- --open
```

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/svelte/installation.md

Install @tanstack/svelte-query package using bun, a fast all-in-one JavaScript runtime and package manager.

```bash

### Run Lit Examples with Custom Ports

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/packages/lit-query/README.md

Commands to run Lit examples, specifying custom ports using environment variables.

```bash
DEMO_PORT=4180 pnpm --dir examples/lit/basic run dev
PAGINATION_DEMO_PORT=4181 PAGINATION_API_PORT=4182 pnpm --dir examples/lit/pagination run dev
SSR_PORT=4180 pnpm --dir examples/lit/ssr run dev
SSR_HOST=0.0.0.0 pnpm --dir examples/lit/ssr run dev
```

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/solid/installation.md

This snippet demonstrates how to install the `@tanstack/solid-query` package, which provides data fetching and state management capabilities for Solid applications. It covers installation using popular JavaScript package managers: npm, pnpm, yarn, and bun. This method is typically used in projects with module bundlers.

```bash
npm i @tanstack/solid-query
```

```bash
pnpm add @tanstack/solid-query
```

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/solid-query
```

```bash
bun add @tanstack/solid-query
```

--------------------------------

### Basic useMutation Hook with Solid.js

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/solid/guides/mutations.md

Demonstrates a simple mutation setup using useMutation with a POST request to create a todo. Shows handling of pending, error, and success states using Solid.js Switch and Show components. The mutation is triggered via button click with mutate method.

```tsx
import { Switch, Match, Show } from 'solid-js'

function App() {
  const mutation = useMutation(() => ({
    mutationFn: (newTodo) => {
      return axios.post('/todos', newTodo)
    },
  }))

  return (
    <div>
      <Switch>
        <Match when={mutation.isPending}>Adding todo...</Match>
        <Match when={true}>
          <Show when={mutation.isError}>
            <div>An error occurred: {mutation.error.message}</div>
          </Show>

          <Show when={mutation.isSuccess}>
            <div>Todo added!</div>
          </Show>

          <button
            onClick={() => {
              mutation.mutate({ id: new Date(), title: 'Do Laundry' })
            }}
          >
            Create Todo
          </button>
        </Match>
      </Switch>
    </div>
  )
}
```

--------------------------------

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/eslint/eslint-plugin-query.md

```bash

```

```bash
pnpm add -D @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query
```

```bash
yarn add -D @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query
```

```bash
bun add -D @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query
```

### Install Lit Query packages

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/installation.md

Install Lit Query, TanStack Query Core, and Lit using your preferred package manager. TanStack Query Core is a peer dependency and must be installed explicitly.

```bash
npm i @tanstack/lit-query @tanstack/query-core lit
```

```bash
pnpm add @tanstack/lit-query @tanstack/query-core lit
```

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/lit-query @tanstack/query-core lit
```

```bash
bun add @tanstack/lit-query @tanstack/query-core lit
```

### Basic useQuery Hook Setup in React

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/guides/queries.md

Demonstrates the minimal setup required to subscribe to a query using the useQuery hook. Requires a unique query key and a promise-returning function. This is the foundation for all query operations in TanStack Query.

```typescript
import { useQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query'

function App() {
  const info = useQuery({ queryKey: ['todos'], queryFn: fetchTodoList })
}
```

--------------------------------

### Example Usage of createQueryController in LitElement (TypeScript)

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/reference/functions/createQueryController.md

This example demonstrates integrating `createQueryController` into a LitElement to manage asynchronous data fetching. It shows how to define a query, access its state, and conditionally render UI based on loading, error, or success states.

```typescript
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import { createQueryController } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

class TodosView extends LitElement {
  private readonly todos = createQueryController(this, {
    queryKey: ['todos'],
    queryFn: async () => fetch('/api/todos').then((r) => r.json()),
  })

  render() {
    const query = this.todos()

    if (query.isPending) return html`Loading...`
    if (query.isError) return html`Error`

    return html`<ul>${query.data.map((todo) => html`<li>${todo.title}</li>`)}</ul>`
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/devtools.md

Install the @tanstack/react-query-devtools package using pnpm package manager.

pnpm add @tanstack/react-query-devtools
```

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/svelte-query

### Install TanStack Query Persist Client Core Package

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/plugins/createPersister.md

```bash
npm install @tanstack/query-persist-client-core
```

```bash
pnpm add @tanstack/query-persist-client-core
```

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/query-persist-client-core
```

```bash
bun add @tanstack/query-persist-client-core
```

```bash

```

```bash

```

```bash

```

```bash

```

### Install TanStack Angular Query Experimental via Package Managers

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/angular/installation.md

This snippet provides commands to install the `@tanstack/angular-query-experimental` library using various package managers. The library is currently in an experimental stage, so users are advised to lock their version to a patch-level to avoid unexpected breaking changes. It is compatible with Angular v16 and higher.

```bash
npm i @tanstack/angular-query-experimental
```

```bash
pnpm add @tanstack/angular-query-experimental
```

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/angular-query-experimental
```

```bash
bun add @tanstack/angular-query-experimental
```

--------------------------------

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/packages/lit-query/README.md

Commands to install dependencies and build the Lit-Query package within the repository for local development.

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm --dir packages/lit-query run build
```

--------------------------------

### Install createSyncStoragePersister packages

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/plugins/createSyncStoragePersister.md

Install the required packages for sync storage persistence in TanStack Query. Multiple package managers are supported including npm, pnpm, yarn, and bun.

```bash
npm install @tanstack/query-sync-storage-persister @tanstack/react-query-persist-client
```

```bash
pnpm add @tanstack/query-sync-storage-persister @tanstack/react-query-persist-client
```

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/query-sync-storage-persister @tanstack/react-query-persist-client
```

```bash
bun add @tanstack/query-sync-storage-persister @tanstack/react-query-persist-client
```

--------------------------------

### Install Vue Query via NPM

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/vue/installation.md

Install @tanstack/vue-query package using npm package manager. This is the standard installation method for Node.js projects.

```bash
npm i @tanstack/vue-query
```

--------------------------------

### Install project dependencies using pnpm

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md

This command installs all necessary project dependencies using the pnpm package manager. It should be run after cloning the repository to set up the development environment.

```bash
pnpm install
```

--------------------------------

### Install Vue Query via yarn

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/vue/installation.md

Install @tanstack/vue-query package using yarn package manager, another popular alternative to npm.

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/vue-query
```

--------------------------------

### Install Vue Query via pnpm

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/vue/installation.md

Install @tanstack/vue-query package using pnpm package manager, an alternative to npm with improved performance.

```bash
pnpm add @tanstack/vue-query
```

--------------------------------

### Install React Query via NPM

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/installation.md

Install @tanstack/react-query package using various Node package managers (npm, pnpm, yarn, or bun). Choose the package manager that matches your project setup.

```bash
npm i @tanstack/react-query
```

```bash
pnpm add @tanstack/react-query
```

```bash
yarn add @tanstack/react-query
```

```bash
bun add @tanstack/react-query
```

--------------------------------

### Using useIsFetching hook in TanStack React Query

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/reference/useIsFetching.md

This example demonstrates how to use the `useIsFetching` hook to get the total number of currently fetching queries, and how to filter by `queryKey` to get the number of fetching queries for a specific group, such as 'posts'.

```tsx
import { useIsFetching } from '@tanstack/react-query'
// How many queries are fetching?
const isFetching = useIsFetching()
// How many queries matching the posts prefix are fetching?
const isFetchingPosts = useIsFetching({ queryKey: ['posts'] })
```

--------------------------------

### Query Key Matching Examples

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/guides/query-invalidation.md

Demonstrates which query keys match a prefix-based invalidation. Both nested and flat keys starting with the prefix are matched.

```typescript
const projectsListKey = ['projects']
const projectsPageKey = ['projects', 1, 250, false]
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize Vue Query with VueQueryPlugin

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/vue/installation.md

Initialize Vue Query in your Vue application by importing VueQueryPlugin and registering it with the Vue app instance. This must be done before using any Vue Query composables.

```typescript
import { VueQueryPlugin } from '@tanstack/vue-query'

app.use(VueQueryPlugin)
```

--------------------------------

### Create SolidStart Project with npm init

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/solid/solid-start-streaming/README.md

Initialize a new SolidStart project using the `npm init solid@latest` command. This command allows creating a project in the current directory or a specified new directory. Ensure `npm` is installed to use this command effectively.

```bash
# create a new project in the current directory
npm init solid@latest

# create a new project in my-app
npm init solid@latest my-app
```

--------------------------------

### Build Production Svelte Application

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/svelte/auto-refetching/README.md

Create an optimized production build of the Svelte application. After building, use npm run preview to test the production version locally before deployment.

```bash
npm run build
```

```bash
npm run preview
```

--------------------------------

### Get all variables of running mutations with useMutationState

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/reference/useMutationState.md

Demonstrates how to use useMutationState to retrieve variables from all mutations with a 'pending' status. This example filters mutations by their status and uses the select function to extract only the variables property from each mutation's state.

```typescript
import { useMutationState } from '@tanstack/react-query'

const variables = useMutationState({
  filters: { status: 'pending' },
  select: (mutation) => mutation.state.variables,
})
```

--------------------------------

### Example: Tracking Todos Fetch Status with useIsFetching

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/reference/functions/useIsFetching.md

Demonstrates how to use `useIsFetching` within a LitElement to display the number of active fetches for a specific query key, such as 'todos'.

```ts
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import { useIsFetching } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

class TodosStatus extends LitElement {
  private readonly todosFetching = useIsFetching(this, {
    queryKey: ['todos'],
  })

  render() {
    return html`<span>${this.todosFetching()} active todo fetches</span>`
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Get mutation data by mutationKey using useMutationState

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/reference/useMutationState.md

Shows how to retrieve data from specific mutations using the mutationKey filter. The example defines a mutation with a specific key and then uses useMutationState to access the state data of that mutation by matching the mutationKey.

```typescript
import { useMutation, useMutationState } from '@tanstack/react-query'

const mutationKey = ['posts']

// Some mutation that we want to get the state for
const mutation = useMutation({
  mutationKey,
  mutationFn: (newPost) => {
    return axios.post('/posts', newPost)
  },
})

const data = useMutationState({
  // this mutation key needs to match the mutation key of the given mutation (see above)
  filters: { mutationKey },
  select: (mutation) => mutation.state.data,
})
```

--------------------------------

### createQueriesController(host, options, queryClient?)

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/reference/functions/createQueriesController.md

Creates a Lit reactive controller that subscribes the host to multiple queries. The returned accessor is callable and also exposes "current" and "destroy". When "options" or "options.queries" is a function, it is re-read during host updates so the query list can follow reactive host state. If "queryClient" is omitted, the controller resolves the client from the nearest connected "QueryClientProvider".

```APIDOC
## Function: createQueriesController()

### Description
Creates a Lit reactive controller that subscribes the host to multiple queries.

### Signature
```ts
function createQueriesController<TQueryOptions, TCombinedResult>(
   host,
   options,
queryClient?): QueriesResultAccessor<TCombinedResult>;
```

### Type Parameters
- **TQueryOptions** *extends* `any`[]
- **TCombinedResult** = `CreateQueriesResults`\<`TQueryOptions`\>

### Parameters
- **host** (`ReactiveControllerHost`) - The Lit reactive controller host that owns the queries subscription.
- **options** ([`Accessor`](../type-aliases/Accessor.md)\<[`CreateQueriesControllerOptions`](../type-aliases/CreateQueriesControllerOptions.md)\<`TQueryOptions`, `TCombinedResult`\>\>) - Queries controller options, or a getter that returns options.
- **queryClient?** (`QueryClient`) - Optional explicit query client. Provide this for controllers that should not resolve a client from Lit context.

### Returns
[`QueriesResultAccessor`](../type-aliases/QueriesResultAccessor.md)\<`TCombinedResult`\> - An accessor for the latest query results, or the value returned by `combine`.

### Example
```ts
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import { createQueriesController } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

class DashboardView extends LitElement {
  private readonly dashboard = createQueriesController(this, {
    queries: [
      { queryKey: ['stats'], queryFn: fetchStats },
      { queryKey: ['projects'], queryFn: fetchProjects },
    ],
    combine: ([stats, projects]) => ({
      stats: stats.data,
      projects: projects.data ?? [],
      isPending: stats.isPending || projects.isPending,
    }),
  })

  render() {
    const dashboard = this.dashboard()
    return html`<p>Projects: ${dashboard.projects.length}</p>`
  }
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### Get Current Online State with TanStack Query's OnlineManager (TypeScript)

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/reference/onlineManager.md

This example demonstrates how to retrieve the current online status as determined by the `OnlineManager`. The `isOnline()` method returns a boolean value, indicating whether the application is currently considered to have an active network connection.

```tsx
const isOnline = onlineManager.isOnline()
```

--------------------------------

### Configure and Use Default Query Function in React

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/solid/guides/default-query-function.md

This example demonstrates how to create a global fetcher using Axios that maps query keys to URL paths. It shows the setup within the QueryClientProvider and how to invoke queries by providing only the query key.

```tsx
// Define a default query function that will receive the query key
const defaultQueryFn = async ({ queryKey }) => {
  const { data } = await axios.get(
    `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com${queryKey[0]}`,
  )
  return data
}

// provide the default query function to your app with defaultOptions
const queryClient = new QueryClient({
  defaultOptions: {
    queries: {
      queryFn: defaultQueryFn,
    },
  },
})

function App() {
  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
      <YourApp />
    </QueryClientProvider>
  )
}

// All you have to do now is pass a key!
function Posts() {
  const postsQuery = useQuery(() => ({ queryKey: ['/posts'] }))

  // ...
}

// You can even leave out the queryFn and just go straight into options
function Post(props) {
  const postQuery = useQuery(() => ({
    queryKey: [`/posts/${props.postId}`],
    enabled: !!props.postId,
  }))

  // ...
}
```

--------------------------------

### Basic Usage of createQueriesController with LitElement (TypeScript)

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/reference/functions/createQueriesController.md

This example demonstrates how to integrate `createQueriesController` into a LitElement. It shows how to define multiple queries and combine their results for display within a Lit component.

```ts
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import { createQueriesController } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

class DashboardView extends LitElement {
  private readonly dashboard = createQueriesController(this, {
    queries: [
      { queryKey: ['stats'], queryFn: fetchStats },
      { queryKey: ['projects'], queryFn: fetchProjects },
    ],
    combine: ([stats, projects]) => ({
      stats: stats.data,
      projects: projects.data ?? [],
      isPending: stats.isPending || projects.isPending,
    }),
  })

  render() {
    const dashboard = this.dashboard()
    return html`<p>Projects: ${dashboard.projects.length}</p>`
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Access latest mutation data via mutationKey with useMutationState

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/reference/useMutationState.md

Demonstrates how to retrieve the most recent mutation data by accessing the last item in the array returned by useMutationState. Each invocation of mutate adds a new entry to the mutation cache for gcTime milliseconds, and this example shows how to get the latest one.

```typescript
import { useMutation, useMutationState } from '@tanstack/react-query'

const mutationKey = ['posts']

// Some mutation that we want to get the state for
const mutation = useMutation({
  mutationKey,
  mutationFn: (newPost) => {
    return axios.post('/posts', newPost)
  },
})

const data = useMutationState({
  // this mutation key needs to match the mutation key of the given mutation (see above)
  filters: { mutationKey },
  select: (mutation) => mutation.state.data,
})

// Latest mutation data
const latest = data[data.length - 1]
```

--------------------------------

### createMutationController(host, options, queryClient?)

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/reference/functions/createMutationController.md

Creates a Lit reactive controller that subscribes the host to a mutation. The returned accessor is callable and also exposes `current`, `mutate`, `mutateAsync`, `reset`, and `destroy`. When `options` is a function, it is re-read during host updates so mutation options can follow reactive host state. If `queryClient` is omitted, the controller resolves the client from the nearest connected `QueryClientProvider`.

```APIDOC
## Function: createMutationController()

### Description
Creates a Lit reactive controller that subscribes the host to a mutation. The returned accessor is callable and also exposes `current`, `mutate`, `mutateAsync`, `reset`, and `destroy`. When `options` is a function, it is re-read during host updates so mutation options can follow reactive host state. If `queryClient` is omitted, the controller resolves the client from the nearest connected `QueryClientProvider`.

### Signature
```ts
function createMutationController<TData, TError, TVariables, TOnMutateResult>(
   host,
   options,
queryClient?): MutationResultAccessor<TData, TError, TVariables, TOnMutateResult>;
```

### Type Parameters
- **TData** (`unknown`)
- **TError** (`Error`)
- **TVariables** (`void`)
- **TOnMutateResult** (`unknown`)

### Parameters
- **host** (`ReactiveControllerHost`) - The Lit reactive controller host that owns the mutation subscription.
- **options** (`Accessor<CreateMutationOptions<TData, TError, TVariables, TOnMutateResult>>`) - Mutation observer options, or a getter that returns options.
- **queryClient?** (`QueryClient`) - Optional explicit query client. Provide this for controllers that should not resolve a client from Lit context.

### Returns
- (`MutationResultAccessor<TData, TError, TVariables, TOnMutateResult>`) - An accessor for the latest mutation result with mutation helper methods.

### Example
```ts
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import { createMutationController } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

class AddTodoForm extends LitElement {
  private readonly addTodo = createMutationController(this, {
    mutationFn: (title: string) =>
      fetch('/api/todos', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ title }) }),
  })

  render() {
    const mutation = this.addTodo()

    return html`
      <button ?disabled=${mutation.isPending} @click=${() => this.addTodo.mutate('Ship docs')}>
        Add todo
      </button>
    `
  }
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### Set up Lit Query with QueryClient and controllers

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/quick-start.md

Initialize a QueryClient, define a QueryClientProvider custom element, and create a component with query and mutation controllers. The controllers subscribe to data changes and trigger updates via the Lit reactive controller lifecycle.

```typescript
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import {
  QueryClient,
  QueryClientProvider,
  createMutationController,
  createQueryController,
} from '@tanstack/lit-query'
import { addTodo, getTodos } from './api'

const queryClient = new QueryClient()

class AppQueryProvider extends QueryClientProvider {
  constructor() {
    super()
    this.client = queryClient
  }
}

customElements.define('app-query-provider', AppQueryProvider)

class TodosView extends LitElement {
  private readonly todos = createQueryController(this, {
    queryKey: ['todos'],
    queryFn: getTodos,
  })

  private readonly createTodo = createMutationController(this, {
    mutationFn: addTodo,
    onSuccess: async () => {
      await queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['todos'] })
    },
  })

  render() {
    const query = this.todos()
    const mutation = this.createTodo()

    if (query.isPending) return html`Loading...`
    if (query.isError) return html`Error: ${query.error.message}`

    return html`
      <ul>
        ${query.data.map((todo) => html`<li>${todo.title}</li>`)}
      </ul>

      <button
        ?disabled=${mutation.isPending}
        @click=${() => this.createTodo.mutate({ title: 'Write Lit docs' })}
      >
        Add Todo
      </button>
    `
  }
}

customElements.define('todos-view', TodosView)
```

--------------------------------

### Start development server for TanStack Query documentation

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md

Launches the development server for the TanStack Query documentation site. This command should be executed in the /docs directory to enable hot-reloading and live preview of documentation changes.

```bash
npm run dev
```

--------------------------------

### createInfiniteQueryController()

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/reference/functions/createInfiniteQueryController.md

Creates a Lit reactive controller that subscribes the host to an infinite query. The returned accessor is callable and also exposes `current`, `refetch`, `fetchNextPage`, `fetchPreviousPage`, and `destroy`. When `options` is a function, it is re-read during host updates so query keys and options can follow reactive host state. If `queryClient` is omitted, the controller resolves the client from the nearest connected `QueryClientProvider`.

```APIDOC
## createInfiniteQueryController()

### Description
Creates a Lit reactive controller that subscribes the host to an infinite query. The returned accessor is callable and also exposes `current`, `refetch`, `fetchNextPage`, `fetchPreviousPage`, and `destroy`. When `options` is a function, it is re-read during host updates so query keys and options can follow reactive host state. If `queryClient` is omitted, the controller resolves the client from the nearest connected `QueryClientProvider`.

### Signature
```ts
function createInfiniteQueryController<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey, TPageParam>(
   host,
   options,
queryClient?): InfiniteQueryResultAccessor<TData, TError>;
```

### Type Parameters
- **TQueryFnData**: `unknown`
- **TError**: `Error`
- **TData**: `InfiniteData<TQueryFnData, unknown>`
- **TQueryKey**: `readonly unknown[]`
- **TPageParam**: `unknown`

### Parameters
- **host** (`ReactiveControllerHost`) - Required - The Lit reactive controller host that owns the infinite query subscription.
- **options** (`Accessor<CreateInfiniteQueryOptions<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey, TPageParam>>`) - Required - Infinite query observer options, or a getter that returns options.
- **queryClient?** (`QueryClient`) - Optional - Optional explicit query client. Provide this for controllers that should not resolve a client from Lit context.

### Returns
`InfiniteQueryResultAccessor<TData, TError>` - An accessor for the latest infinite query result with page helper methods.

### Example
```ts
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import { createInfiniteQueryController } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

class ProjectsView extends LitElement {
  private readonly projects = createInfiniteQueryController(this, {
    queryKey: ['projects'],
    queryFn: ({ pageParam }) => fetchProjects(pageParam),
    initialPageParam: 0,
    getNextPageParam: (lastPage) => lastPage.nextCursor,
  })

  render() {
    const query = this.projects()

    return html`
      <button ?disabled=${!query.hasNextPage} @click=${() => this.projects.fetchNextPage()}>
        Load more
      </button>
    `
  }
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### Registering QueryClientProvider directly

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/reference/classes/QueryClientProvider.md

This example demonstrates registering `QueryClientProvider` directly as a custom element and binding the `QueryClient` using property binding in a Lit template.

```ts
import { html } from 'lit'
import { QueryClient, QueryClientProvider } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

const queryClient = new QueryClient()

customElements.define('query-client-provider', QueryClientProvider)

const view = html`
  <query-client-provider .client=${queryClient}>
    <todos-view></todos-view>
  </query-client-provider>
`
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize a Lit Query Controller

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/lit/guides/queries.md

Demonstrates how to create and use `createQueryController` within a LitElement to fetch and display data, handling pending and error states.

```ts
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit'
import { createQueryController } from '@tanstack/lit-query'

class TodosView extends LitElement {
  private readonly todos = createQueryController(this, {
    queryKey: ['todos'],
    queryFn: fetchTodos,
  })

  render() {
    const query = this.todos()

    if (query.isPending) return html`Loading...`
    if (query.isError) return html`Error: ${query.error.message}`

    return html`
      <ul>
        ${query.data.map((todo) => html`<li>${todo.title}</li>`)}
      </ul>
    `
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Incorrect Property Order in TanStack `useInfiniteQuery` (TSX)

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/eslint/infinite-query-property-order.md

This example illustrates an incorrect property order when defining an infinite query using `useInfiniteQuery` in TanStack Query. Specifically, `getNextPageParam` and `getPreviousPageParam` are placed before and after `queryFn` respectively, which can hinder proper type inference. This setup fetches paginated project data but violates the recommended property order for inference-sensitive properties.

```tsx
/* eslint "@tanstack/query/infinite-query-property-order": "warn" */
import { useInfiniteQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query'

const query = useInfiniteQuery({
  queryKey: ['projects'],
  getNextPageParam: (lastPage) => lastPage.nextId ?? undefined,
  queryFn: async ({ pageParam }) => {
    const response = await fetch(`/api/projects?cursor=${pageParam}`)
    return await response.json()
  },
  initialPageParam: 0,
  getPreviousPageParam: (firstPage) => firstPage.previousId ?? undefined,
  maxPages: 3,
})
```

--------------------------------

### Nesting Server Components with Individual QueryClients

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/guides/advanced-ssr.md

Demonstrates how to structure nested Server Components, each prefetching data with its own `QueryClient` and `HydrationBoundary`.

```tsx
// app/posts/page.tsx
import {
  dehydrate,
  HydrationBoundary,
  QueryClient,
} from '@tanstack/react-query'
import Posts from './posts'
import CommentsServerComponent from './comments-server'

export default async function PostsPage() {
  const queryClient = new QueryClient()

  await queryClient.prefetchQuery({
    queryKey: ['posts'],
    queryFn: getPosts,
  })

  return (
    <HydrationBoundary state={dehydrate(queryClient)}>
      <Posts />
      <CommentsServerComponent />
    </HydrationBoundary>
  )
}

// app/posts/comments-server.tsx
import {
  dehydrate,
  HydrationBoundary,
  QueryClient,
} from '@tanstack/react-query'
import Comments from './comments'

export default async function CommentsServerComponent() {
  const queryClient = new QueryClient()

  await queryClient.prefetchQuery({
    queryKey: ['posts-comments'],
    queryFn: getComments,
  })

  return (
    <HydrationBoundary state={dehydrate(queryClient)}>
      <Comments />
    </HydrationBoundary>
  )
}
```

--------------------------------

### Install ESLint Plugin Query via NPM

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/installation.md

Install @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query as a development dependency using various package managers. This plugin helps catch bugs and inconsistencies in React Query code during development.

```bash
npm i -D @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query
```

```bash
pnpm add -D @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query
```

```bash
yarn add -D @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query
```

```bash
bun add -D @tanstack/eslint-plugin-query
```

--------------------------------

### Prefetching Article Comments using useQuery in Solid.js

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/solid/guides/prefetching.md

This example demonstrates how to prefetch related data (article comments) within a component using `useQuery` itself, but ignoring its result. This is an optimization to ensure data is available when the `Comments` component eventually renders, improving perceived performance. It also shows `notifyOnChangeProps: []` for further optimization.

```tsx
import { Switch, Match } from 'solid-js'

function Article(props) {
  const articleQuery = useQuery(() => ({
    queryKey: ['article', props.id],
    queryFn: getArticleById,
  }))

  // Prefetch
  useQuery(() => ({
    queryKey: ['article-comments', props.id],
    queryFn: getArticleCommentsById,
    // Optional optimization to avoid rerenders when this query changes:
    notifyOnChangeProps: [],
  }))

  return (
    <Switch>
      <Match when={articleQuery.isPending}>
        Loading article...
      </Match>
      <Match when={articleQuery.isSuccess}>
        <ArticleHeader articleData={articleQuery.data} />
        <ArticleBody articleData={articleQuery.data} />
        <Comments id={props.id} />
      </Match>
    </Switch>
  )
}

function Comments(props) {
  const commentsQuery = useQuery(() => ({
    queryKey: ['article-comments', props.id],
    queryFn: getArticleCommentsById,
  }))

  ...
}
```

--------------------------------

### Setting up QueryClientProvider for Server Components in Next.js

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/react/guides/advanced-ssr.md

This client-side provider sets up a `QueryClient` instance, ensuring a new client is created on the server and a singleton is reused in the browser to prevent re-initialization during initial renders.

```tsx
// In Next.js, this file would be called: app/providers.tsx
'use client'

// Since QueryClientProvider relies on useContext under the hood, we have to put 'use client' on top
import {
  isServer,
  QueryClient,
  QueryClientProvider,
} from '@tanstack/react-query'

function makeQueryClient() {
  return new QueryClient({
    defaultOptions: {
      queries: {
        // With SSR, we usually want to set some default staleTime
        // above 0 to avoid refetching immediately on the client
        staleTime: 60 * 1000,
      },
    },
  })
}

let browserQueryClient: QueryClient | undefined = undefined

function getQueryClient() {
  if (isServer) {
    // Server: always make a new query client
    return makeQueryClient()
  } else {
    // Browser: make a new query client if we don't already have one
    // This is very important, so we don't re-make a new client if React
    // suspends during the initial render. This may not be needed if we
    // have a suspense boundary BELOW the creation of the query client
    if (!browserQueryClient) browserQueryClient = makeQueryClient()
    return browserQueryClient
  }
}

export default function Providers({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  // NOTE: Avoid useState when initializing the query client if you don't
  //       have a suspense boundary between this and the code that may
  //       suspend because React will throw away the client on the initial
  //       render if it suspends and there is no boundary
  const queryClient = getQueryClient()

  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>{children}</QueryClientProvider>
  )
}
```

--------------------------------

### Reactive useQuery Options with Solid Signals

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/docs/framework/solid/reference/useQuery.md

Shows how to use reactive options with `useQuery` by making the `queryKey` and `queryFn` dependent on a SolidJS signal.

```tsx
import { useQuery } from '@tanstack/solid-query'

function App() {
  const [filter, setFilter] = createSignal('all')

  const todos = useQuery(() => ({
    queryKey: ['todos', filter()],
    queryFn: async () => {
      const response = await fetch(`/api/todos?filter=${filter()}`)
      if (!response.ok) {
        throw new Error('Failed to fetch todos')
      }
      return response.json()
    },
  }))

  return (
    <div>
      <div>
        <button onClick={() => setFilter('all')}>All</button>
        <button onClick={() => setFilter('active')}>Active</button>
        <button onClick={() => setFilter('completed')}>Completed</button>
      </div>
      <Show when={todos.isError}>
        <div>Error: {todos.error.message}</div>
      </Show>
      <Show when={todos.isLoading}>
        <div>Loading...</div>
      </Show>
      <Show when={todos.isSuccess}>
        <div>
          <div>Todos:</div>
          <ul>
            <For each={todos.data}>{(todo) => <li>{todo.title}</li>}</For>
          </ul>
        </div>
      </Show>
    </div>
  )
}
```

--------------------------------

### Build Nuxt 3 application for production

Source: https://github.com/tanstack/query/blob/main/examples/vue/nuxt3/README.md

Create an optimized production build of the Nuxt 3 application. See deployment documentation for hosting options.

```bash
pnpm build
```
